coredata.dc:title: Investigating of the anticancer activity of salen/salophen metal complexes based on graphene quantum dots: Induction of apoptosis as part of biological activity
coredata.dc:creator: Nejad, Zahra Kiani
Khandar, Ali Akbar
Khatamian, Massoumeh
Ghorbani, Marjan
coredata.dc:description: This research work is the first report on the synthesis and stabilization of [Fe-Salophen] and [Fe-Salen] complexes by two methods of surface modification and anchoring of synthesized Schiff base ligand on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The GQDs contain oxygenated functional groups that can act as non-radiative electron-hole recombination centers. Therefore removing these oxygen functional groups may improve quantum yield by reducing or deactivating the surface. In this work, GQDs with the amine functional group were synthesized with a quantum yield of 37.48%. The physicochemical properties of GQDs were investigated by Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectroscopies, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), Transmission electron microscope (TEM). The synthesis of GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] was evaluated by FT-IR, Inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) and Energy dispersive X-Ray analysis (EDX) analyses. Then, using MTT- assay, annexin V-FITC/PI, DAPI staining and cellular uptake assays, the biochemical activity of these complexes on the MCF7 cell line was investigated. The results shows that GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] affect the survival of MCF7 cancer cells and, by nuclear fragmentation cause 35.77% and 19.41% of early apoptosis in cells, respectively. Also was found cellular uptake of GQDs-[Fe-Salen] is higher than that of GQDs-[Fe-Salophen].
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Cellular uptake
Cell survival
Apoptosis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Amination of graphene quantum dots (GQDs-NH2) to the first method
Preparation of Schiff base ligand on the GQDs-NH2 to the first method
Preparation of ligands N, Ń-bis(4-hydroxysalicylidene) ethylene-1,2-diamine, H2 [ (OH)2-Salen], and N, Ń-bis(4-hydroxysalycilidene)-1,2-phenylenediamine, H2[(OH)2-Salophen] to the second method
Anchoring the H2 [(OH)2-Salen)] and H2 [(OH)2-Salophen] ligands on GQDs to the second method
Grafte of Fe (III) to Schiff base ligand
Investigation of the binding of amine functional groups on GQDs
Evaluation of cell apoptosis by flow cytometry
Assay of cell apoptosis by DAPI staining
Investigating cell internalization by fluorescence microscopy
Investigating cell internalization by flow cytometry
Study of cell apoptosis in vitro
Cell apoptosis assay by DAPI staining
Investigation of cell uptake in vitro

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: GQDs emission spectra at different wavelengths of excitation.
XPS spectrum of (A) GQDs, (B) the microstructure of C1s, (C) the microstructure of O1s.
PXRD diffraction pattern of (A) GQDs, (B) GQDs-NH2.
Images from the scattering of elements throughout particles: (A) GQDs, (B) GQDs-NH2.
Qualitative detection amination of GQDs using the ninhydrin test, Inset; (upper) GQDs-NH2 before and after reaction with ninhydrin.
(A, B) FESEM micrograph of GQDs, (C) GQDs-[Fe-salen], (D) GQDs-[Fe-salophen].

UV–Vis absorption spectra of (A) GQDs, (B) GQDs-NH2, (C) GQDs-SB, (D) GQDs-[Fe-salophen], (E) GQDs-[Fe-salen], inset: (upper) GQDs aqueous solution under visible light and UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm.
FT-IR spectra of (A) GQDs, (B) GQDs-Cl, (C) GQDs-NH2, (D) GQDs-SB, (E) GQDs-[Fe-salen], (F) GQDs-PhNH2, (G) GQDs-SB, (H) GQDs-[Fe-salophen].
FT-IR spectrum of (A) GQDs, (B) GQDs-Cl, (C) GQDs-[(OH)2-salen], (D)GQDs-[Fe(O)2-salen], (E)GQDs-[(OH)2-salophen], (F) GQDs-[Fe(O)2-salophen].

The survival percentage of MCF7 cells in the presence of different concentrations (A) GQDs-[Fe-salen], (B) GQDs-[Fe-salophen] after 24 and 48 h of incubation (values represent the average of three experiments).
Flow cytometry analysis with annexin V-FITC/PI on MCF7 cells treated by (A) GQDs-[Fe-salen], (B) GQDs-[Fe-salophen], (control: untreated MCF7 cells).
Evaluation of apoptosis induction by DAPI staining of MCF7 cells treated by (A) GQDs-[Fe-salen], (B) GQDs-[Fe-salophen] (control: untreated MCF7 cells).

Fluorescence microscope images of cellular uptake of rhodamine B-labeled samples by MCF7 cells: (A) GQDs-[Fe-salen] after 1 h, (B) GQDs-[Fe-salen] after 4 h, (C) GQDs-[Fe-salophen] after 1 h, (D) GQDs-[Fe-salophen] after 4 h.
Cell uptake study by flow cytometry after treatment of MCF7 cells by (A)GQDs-[Fe-salen], (B)GQDs-[Fe-salophen] after 4 h of incubation (control: untreated MCF7 cells).
Schematic illustration of synthesis and stabilization [Fe-salen] on GQDs.
Anti-proliferative activities (IC50) of GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] nanoparticles against MCF7 cell line after 24 h treatment.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: IC50 represents the concentration at which compounds exerts half of its maximal inhibitory effect. MCF7 cancer cells were treated with compounds for 24 h. IC50 values as mean ± SEM (standard error of the mean), n = 3.

head.ce:title: Investigating of the anticancer activity of salen/salophen metal complexes based on graphene quantum dots: Induction of apoptosis as part of biological activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nutrition Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nutrition Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research work is the first report on the synthesis and stabilization of [Fe-Salophen] and [Fe-Salen] complexes by two methods of surface modification and anchoring of synthesized Schiff base ligand on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The GQDs contain oxygenated functional groups that can act as non-radiative electron-hole recombination centers. Therefore removing these oxygen functional groups may improve quantum yield by reducing or deactivating the surface. In this work, GQDs with the amine functional group were synthesized with a quantum yield of 37.48%. The physicochemical properties of GQDs were investigated by Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) and fluorescence spectroscopies, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), Transmission electron microscope (TEM). The synthesis of GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] was evaluated by FT-IR, Inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) and Energy dispersive X-Ray analysis (EDX) analyses. Then, using MTT- assay, annexin V-FITC/PI, DAPI staining and cellular uptake assays, the biochemical activity of these complexes on the MCF7 cell line was investigated. The results shows that GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] affect the survival of MCF7 cancer cells and, by nuclear fragmentation cause 35.77% and 19.41% of early apoptosis in cells, respectively. Also was found cellular uptake of GQDs-[Fe-Salen] is higher than that of GQDs-[Fe-Salophen].

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon nanomaterials with fluorescence properties that were discovered first in 2004 during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (Niu et al., 2015). Therefore, carbon compounds with a size of less than 10 nm were introduced as CQDs (Paulo et al., 2016). CQDs, due to unique properties such as intrinsic fluorescence, low toxicity, high solubility in water, high bio-compatibility, high chemical stability and many other applications have the attention of scientists in recent years (Li, 2012; Ming, 2012; Shen, 2012) (see Scheme 1 ).

Recently, another group of carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene oxide (GO) and GQDs with luminescent properties, has appeared that have attracted more attention (Eda et al., 2010; Loh et al., 2010). The word GQDs does not actually represent one layer of the graphene network but represents of one to ten graphene layers with a diameter of less than 20 nm (Dong et al., 2012a). Most synthesized GQDs are in the form of elliptical or circular. Recently, GQDs have been well synthesized in the form of triangles, quadrilaterals, and hexagons (Zheng et al., 2015). Today, GQDs are due to unique properties such as large surface area, high thermal conductivity, low cell toxicity, better chemical bonding surface due to their π-π conjugate networks, functional groups on their surface, catalytic properties, high fluorescence activity, high optical stability, high solubility in water and adjustable band gap, in the field of nanotechnology have attracted much attention (Liu et al., 2011; Pan et al., 2012; Li et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2008; Robinson et al., 2011; Yan et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2013). GQDs are synthesized in two main ways: the bottom-up and the top-down approach. In the bottom-up method, nanoparticles are prepared by pyrolysis, combustion or hydrothermal of small organic molecules. However, in the top-down method the nanoparticles of the desired size are prepared by crushing small sheets by physical, chemical, or electrochemical methods (Zhao et al., 2017).

In recent years, chemotherapy for cancer has become very important. The first metal-based anti-cancer drugs included cisplatin, carboplatin, and oxaliplatin, which are platinum-based drugs. Gradually, the synthesis of other complexes of metals was begun and underwent clinical trials. Metalosalens are metal complexes whose ligands are salen, complexes, due to their structural properties show a wide range of chemical activities and are widely used to catalyze organic reactions. The salen metal complexes shows have the potential capability of antioxidant, anti-cancer and therapeutic. This group of complexes sometimes acts as chemical nucleases and, by binding to nucleic acids, destroy them. Therefore, they have known as useful probes in biological research and potential therapist factors (Ansari et al., 2009; Beshir et al., 2008). The salen ligands have two covalent coordination positions in their structure. Salen ligands are very similar to porphyrins, but unlike porphyrins salen ligands are prepared easy and inexpensive (Atwood and Harvey, 2001). The metal complexes of these ligands are used in many important industrial and pharmaceutical reactions (Annis and Jacobsen, 1999). Many of the salens complexes, due to the lack of sufficient polar and ionic groups are insoluble in the water. The first water-soluble complexes were synthesized in 1955 and 1956 (Indyk et al., 1999; Konsler et al., 1998; Larrow and Jacobsen, 2004). We expect salophen complexes show similar properties to salen complexes.

The potential and application of stabilized metal complexes depend on the structural nature of the substrate (Zhao et al., 2014). Among the various candidates for nanocarriers of chemotherapeutic agents, GQDs have attracted a lot of attention. Their outstanding feature is related to the next zero layer and oxygenated functional groups at the edges, which makes them ideal for loading hydrophobic anti-cancer drugs and good stability in the body. Another outstanding feature of GQDs is their fluorescence origin. GQDs are an ideal platform for delivering traceable chemotherapeutic agents to cancer cells without the use of fluorescence markers due to the quantum range and the effects of the edge (Fan et al., 2017; Kong et al., 2012; Tang et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2011; Yang et al., 2013). In this work, GQDs were synthesized using adjust of the degree of carbonization of citric acid as a precursor (bottom-up method). The synthesized GQDs have blue photoluminescence. After modification of the surface of GQDs, complexes [Fe-Salen] and [Fe-Salophen] were synthesized on the surface of the modified GQDs. In the next step, their anti-cancer property on MCF7 cancer cells was studied.

All chemicals and reagents include: citric acid, thionyl chloride (SOCl2), ethylene diamine (EDA), absolute ethanol, salicylic aldehyde, 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde, 1,2-phenylenediamine (OPD), Fe(Cl)3·6H2O from Merck Chemical Co. (Dermasdat, Germany) were prepared.

FTIR spectra were recorded by FTIR spectrometer (Tensor 27) using a KBr disk. UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained with the UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-160 A). A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu RF-540) was used to record the emission spectrum. Also, PXRD analysis in the range of 2ɵ= 8˚-70˚ degrees by XRD (Philips PW1730) was performed with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54056) and device step of 0.05 degrees per second. Surface morphology study, EDX analysis to determine the elements present in the sample and preparation of element scattering map on the sample surface (x-ray map) with FE-SEM TESCAN MIRA3 equipped with X-ray analysis system of the type EDS was performed. TEM images were recorded with TEM device of Philips EM 208S. The amount of Fe (III) coordinated to the synthesized ligand on the modified GQDs was determined by ICP-OES spectroscopy (Perkin-Elmer DV5300). The XPS spectrum was measured by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha + ) using Al-Kα radiation.

Preparation of the ligands salen and salophen was performed by two methods. In one method, ligands were synthesized directly on the functionalized surface GQDs, but in the other method, ligands were prepared separately by the condensation reaction of 2,4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde and EDA (or OPD). Then ligands salen and salophen were stabilized on the surface of functionalized GQDs, as shown in schematically 1 and described in the following section.

Citric acid pyrolysis (bottom-up method) was used to prepare GQDs. Briefly, the ceramic container containing citric acid (2 g, 10.41 mmol) was heated in an oven at 200 °C for 20 min. Citric acid appeared as a colorless, then pale yellow viscous liquid, changing color from yellow to orange within 15 min. Finally, by adding viscous orange liquid to sodium hydroxide solution (0.025 M) and adjusting the pH in 7.0, a solution of GQDs was obtained (Dong et al., 2012b). After removing the solvent by vacuum distillation, a yellow powder was obtained.

For the synthesis of chlorinated graphene quantum dots (GQDs-Cl), first, 200 mg of GQDs in 40 ml of SOCl2 was dispersed by ultrasonic. Then, the resulting mixture was refluxed for 72 h at 80 °C under nitrogen gas. The precipitate, after separation by centrifugation and washed with dry THF, was dried in a vacuum desiccator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amination of graphene quantum dots (GQDs-NH2) to the first method
ce:section.ce:para: 200 mg of GQDs-Cl in 60 ml of ethylene diamine was dispersed by ultrasonic for 10 min. Then, 0.2 ml of triethylamine was added to the mixture as an accelerant and the suspension was refluxed for 48 h at 120 °C. The resulting white precipitate was separated by centrifugation and was dried after washing with acetone and ethanol for 24 h in a vacuum desiccator at 80 °C (quantum yield 37.48%). Amination of GQDs with OPD and preparation GQDs-PhNH2 was performed as follows: 300 mg of OPD was dissolved in a mixture of 20 ml of absolute ethanol and 6 ml of triethylamine. Then, 100 mg of GQDs-Cl was added to the solution and the mixture was refluxed for 96 h at 90 °C. The precipitate was washed with acetone and ethanol and dried in a vacuum desiccator at 80 °C (quantum yield 28.71%).Also, based on elemental analysis, the percentage of nitrogen placed on the sample GQDs-NH2 and GQDs-PhNH2 was determined to be 4.8 mmol/g and 4.0 mmol/g respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Schiff base ligand on the GQDs-NH2 to the first method
ce:section.ce:para: To stabilize the Schiff base ligand on the GQDs substrate, 200 mg of white GQDs-NH2 precipitate was dispersed by ultrasonic in 40 ml of absolute ethanol. Then, 4 ml of salicylaldehyde and a few drops of acetic acid were added to the mixture. The yellow color of the solution indicates the formation of an immine bond (Azar et al., 2015). The resulting suspension was refluxed for 24 h at 80 °C under nitrogen gas. After cooling the sample to room temperature, the yellow precipitate was separated by centrifugation and was dried in a vacuum desiccator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ligands N, Ń-bis(4-hydroxysalicylidene) ethylene-1,2-diamine, H2 [ (OH)2-Salen], and N, Ń-bis(4-hydroxysalycilidene)-1,2-phenylenediamine, H2[(OH)2-Salophen] to the second method
ce:section.ce:para: N, Ń-bis(4-hydroxysalicylidene) ethylene-1,2-diamine and N, Ń-bis(4-hydroxysalycilidene)-1,2-phenylenediamine ligands were prepared according to method (Salavati-Niasari et al., 2011). Briefly, 1.1 g (0.008 mol) of 2, 4-dihydroxybenzaldehyde in 20 ml of ethanol was dissolved. Then, the mixture of 0.27 ml (0.004 mol) of ethylene diamine (or 0.004 mol of 1, 2-phenylenediamine) and 1 ml of triethylamine in 20 ml of ethanol was added to the solution. The sample was refluxed for 3 h at 90 °C. The precipitate of yellow (greenish-yellow) obtained after separation by filtration and washing with ethanol and CH2Cl2 was dried in a vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anchoring the H2 [(OH)2-Salen)] and H2 [(OH)2-Salophen] ligands on GQDs to the second method
ce:section.ce:para: For anchoring the [(OH)2-salen)] and [(OH)2-salophen] ligands to the GQDs, 200 mg of GQDs-Cl were dispersed in 30 ml of ethanol. 400 mg of the ligand and 1 ml of triethylamine as an accelerant were added to the suspension. The sample was refluxed for 48 h at 90 °C under nitrogen gas. The precipitate was separated by filtration and, after several times washing with THF and CH2Cl2, dried in a vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Grafte of Fe (III) to Schiff base ligand
ce:section.ce:para: 100 mg GQDs-[(O)2-salen] (or GQDs-[(O)2-salophen]) was dispersed by ultrasonic in 20 ml of absolute ethanol. Then 300 mg of Fe(Cl)3 dissolved in 15 ml of absolute ethanol and was added to the mixture. The resulting suspension was refluxed for 24 h at 90 °C. The resulting mixture was centrifuged. The resulting precipitate was washed thoroughly with absolute ethanol and dried in a vacuum desiccator. The metal content coordinated to the ligand was determined by ICP-OES. The amount of Fe (III) coordinated to the Schiff base ligand synthesized on the surface of GQDs by methods of surface modification and stabilization of Schiff base ligand is 3.6 mmol.g−1, 3.5 mmol.g−1, 3.0 mmol.g−1, and 2.9 mmol.g−1, respectively.

According to the results of ICP-OES analysis, it was found that more metal ions are coordinated to the ligands synthesized by the surface modification method on graphene quantum dots than to the synthetic ligands stabilized on graphene quantum dots (anchoring method). Therefore, only the biological properties of the respective metal complexes (surface modification method) have been evaluated.

To measure quantum yield, quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 with a quantum yield of 0.54 was used as standard. First, the emission of standard and GQDs-NH2 was measured at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, then the absorption of the main and standard samples was read at the same wavelength. Finally, using equation (1), quantum yield was calculated.
(1)
ϕ X
=
ϕ R
I X
/
I R
η s 2
/
η w 2
A R
/
A X

In this relation φ, I and A are the quantum yield, the integrated emission intensity and the absorption of the sample (X), standard (R), respectively. ηs and ηw are the H2SO4 and water refractive index, respectively (Wang et al., 2013; Hu and Liu, 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the binding of amine functional groups on GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the binding of amine functional groups on GQDs ninhydrin test was used. For this purpose, a sample containing 10 mg of GQDs-NH2, 2 ml of phosphate buffer, 4 ml of 1% ninhydrin was prepared in phosphate buffer. The mixture was heated for 10 min in an oven at 100 °C. After cooling the sample at room temperature, the sample was centrifuged for 5 min and adsorption of the supernatant solution was read by UV–Vis spectroscopy. The change in color of the solution from yellow to purple and the adsorption peak at 570 nm indicates the presence of the amine functional group in the sample.

The inhibitory effect of GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] on MCF7cancer cells growth was assessed using MTT assay. MCF7 cancer cells with a density of at 2 × 105 per well were cultured in 96 well plates and RPMI 1640 culture medium containing 10% bovine fetal serum (FBS) penicillin and streptomycin. To achieve the desired congestion, plate was incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and in the atmosphere 5% CO2. The supernatant solution of wells was emptied, after the cells adhered to the bottom of the wells and stabilized. Then the cancer cells inside the wells were treated with different concentrations of GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] (0, 15.62, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250 µg/ml). After 24 h and 48 h of incubation, the culture medium was drained and 200 μl of dye MTT (concentration of 5 mg/ml) dissolved in PBS buffer was added to the wells and the plate was incubated again for 4 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2 atmosphere. The culture medium was then discarded and 200 μl of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well to dissolve insoluble formazan crystals. Finally, after 10 min, solution optical absorption was read at 570 nm. The ratio of optical absorption of treated cancer cells to the control group (untreated cells) indicates the percentage of cell viability (Hamishehkar and Bahadori, 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cell apoptosis by flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare the cells to study the mechanism of cell death by flow cytometry, The MCF7 cancer cells at a density of 1 × 104 cells/each well, were cultured into 24-well plates. The cultured cells were incubated for 48 h after treatment with IC50 concentrations of GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen]. In order to collect the cells, samples were centrifuged at the end of the incubation time for 5 min and then the supernatant solution was discarded. The cells were dissolved in 500 μl of binding buffer and 5 μl of annexin V-FITC and 5 μl of propidium iodide (50 mg/ml) were added to this solution. The cells were then incubated for 5 min at room temperature and in the dark and finally analyzed by flow cytometry device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of cell apoptosis by DAPI staining
ce:section.ce:para: To this purpose, the MCF7cells (2 × 105 cell) were cultured on a glass sheets existent in each well of the 6-well plates. For the complete culture of the cells, the plate was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. After the treatment of cells by GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen], the plate was incubated for 48 h. After the end incubation, the culture medium was drained and the cells were washed with PBS. The cells were fixed by adding paraformaldehyde 4% to each well and placing the plate for 10 min at 37 °C. Then paraformaldehyde was removed and the cells were washed with PBS. In the next step, triton X-100 solution (0.1%) was added to the wells and for greater permeability, incubation was performed for 5 min. Cells were stained with DAPI solution (0.1%) after washing with PBS buffer. After staining the cells, additional dye with PBS was washed. Finally, apoptotic nuclei were imaged using a fluorescent microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating cell internalization by fluorescence microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: A qualitative study of cell uptake was performed using a fluorescent microscope. In summary, MCF7 cells (50,000 cell) were cultured in a 6-well plate, and the plate was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h to stabilize the cells. Then, the cells were treated with GQDs-[Fe-Salen)] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] labeled with rhodamine B for 1 h and 4 h. In the end, the collected cells were washed with phosphate buffer (PBS), and the cell uptake of the samples was measured by fluorescent microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigating cell internalization by flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: First, GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] were labeled with rhodamine B and then their cell uptake was evaluated using flow cytometry. For this purpose, MCF7 cells with a density of 5 × 104 cell were cultured in a 6-well plate, and the plate was incubated for 24 h. The cells were incubated for 4 h at 37 °C after treatment with the labeled samples. After 4 h, the culture medium overflowed and the cells were washed with PBS (pH 7.4). Eventually, cell uptake was measured by flow cytometry.

Oxygenated functional groups cause fluorescent properties in GQDs. Fig. 1 shows the emission spectrum of GQDs in aqueous solution. λMax, ex and λMax, em equal 360 and 460 nm, respectively. When GQDs are excited in the range of 320–440 nm, the emission wavelength is independent of the excitation wavelength. Dependence on excitation wavelength may be due to from emitting sites of GQDs by different sizes and the independence of the emission wavelength from the excitation wavelength is probably achieved when the GQDs have the same size and emission locations (Li et al., 2013).

To further investigate the chemical composition and determine the type of bond at GQDs, XPS analysis was performed. The XPS spectrum of GQDs is shown in Fig. 2 A. The high-resolution C1s spectrum is shown in Fig. 2B. The peaks appearing in 285.18 eV, 285.38 eV, 287.15 eV, 288/68 eV and 288/98 eV are related to the orbital energy of C1s at C–C, C=C, C–O, C=O and O–C=O, respectively. Also, 531.68 eV, 531.98 eV and 536.58 eV peaks in the high resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) belong to the orbital energy of O1s at C=O, C–OH and O=C–OH (Li et al., 2013).
TEM images GQDs (Fig. 3 ) show spherically shaped particles and dispersed, and no aggregation has occurred during synthesis.

The graphite structure of GQDs was investigated by PXRD analysis. As can be seen in Fig. 4 , the PXRD pattern of GQDs in the range 2θ = 870° shows a wide peak at 2θ = 28.44° with d-spacing of 0.314 nm (Li and Ren, 2014). PXRD pattern of GQDs-NH2 show peaks at 2θ = 10.95° and 22.11° belong to plates 001 and 002 with d-spacing 0.81 nm and 0.403 nm, respectively. The increase of interplane spacing in GQDs-NH2 compared to GQDs can be attributed to the presence of amine functional groups.
The scattering images of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements of GQDs and GQDs-NH2 shown in Fig. 5 .

The ninhydrin test was used to investigate the binding of amine functional groups on GQDs. The appearance of violet color is as a result from a reaction between ninhydrin and GQDs-NH2 also confirms the presence of amine functional groups on the GQDs (Fig. 6 ).

The changes made in the morphology of GQDs as a result of the immobilized of [Fe-Salen] and [Fe-Salophen] complexes on it were investigated by FESEM. FESEM images in Fig. 7 A and 7B show that the GQDs have a spherical structure with an average size of 11 nm. The increase in particle size confirms the immobilization of [Fe-Salen] and [Fe-Salophen] complexes on GQDs Fig. 7C and 7D.

Measuring the amount of ultraviolet radiation absorption by a compound is a simple method of studying structural changes (Lv, 2017). GQDs have optical absorption in the ultraviolet region. The UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs shows a strong absorption peak at 360 nm, which is related to n-π* transitions (Pan, 2015). The GQDs aqueous solution appears under visible light in yellow color and under a UV lamp with a wavelength of 365 nm in blue color (Dong et al., 2012b). Fig. 8 shows the UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs, GQDs-NH2, and ligand immobilized on GQDs (GQDs -SB), GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] complexes. The characteristic peak of Schiff ligand at 331 nm is related to n → π* transitions of azomethine (–C=N). In the absorption spectrum of GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] complexes, the strong band is assigned to the ligand to metal charge transfer (LMCT) and the weaker band is assigned to the d-d transitions. The absorption band of the complexes is shifted to higher wavelengths compared to the Schiff base ligand. This displacement indicates the coordination of metal ions to the ligand.

The functional groups of the GQDs, GQDs-Cl, GQDs-NH2, GQDs-PhNH2, GQDs-SB, GQDs-[Fe-salen] and GQDs-[salophen] synthesized were identified by FT-IR technique (Fig. 9 ). In the IR spectrum of GQDs, peaks observed at 1249 cm−1, 2851 cm−1 and 2922 cm−1 are corresponded to stretching vibrations of C-OH and C–H bonds, respectively. The observation of the C–H bonds indicates incomplete carbonization of citric acid (Dong et al., 2012b). The bands appearing in 1387 cm−1, 1630 cm−1 and 3429 cm−1 are attributed to the C=C of the aromatic rings, C=O and O–H vibrations, respectively (Fig. 9A). After treatment of GQDs with SOCl2, the carboxylic acid functional groups were converted to acyl chloride. The Cl atom of the acyl chloride group shifts the vibrational frequency –C=O from 1630 cm−1 to 1711 cm−1. The peak that appeared in 623 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibrations of the C–Cl groups (Fig. 9B). In the FT-IR spectrum of Fig. 9C, the vibrations C–N, C=O and N–H (amine) are observed at 1262 cm−1, 1590 cm−1 and 3176 cm−1, respectively. Peaks 2817 cm−1 and 2927 cm−1 related to C–H bonds also confirm the successful formation of GQDs-NH2 (Sun, 2013). The observation of the C=N vibration band in 1601 cm−1 and the disappearance of the N–H band of Fig. 9C, confirm the synthesis and stabilization of the Schiff base ligand on the substrate (Fig. 9D). The shift of the C=N vibrational band to a lower frequency (∼11 cm−1) may be attributed to the coordination of the N atom with Fe ion (Fig. 9E). The N–H vibration band of GQDs-PhNH2 overlaps with C–H vibration band (Fig. 9F). The Peaks 1258 cm−1 and 1601 cm−1 in Fig. 9F are attributed to the C–N and C=O bonds, respectively. In the GQDs-SB spectrum, the band 1607 cm−1 is the characteristic peak of C=N, which confirm the formation of Schiff base on the GQDs (Fig. 9G). Comparison of the Fig. 9G and Fig. 9H spectra shows that the peak attributed to C=N at 1607 cm−1 (Fig. 9G) is shifted to lower frequency (1598 cm−1) as soon as the formation of a complex with Fe (III) ion.

To confirm the covalent anchoring of Schiff base ligands [(OH)2-salen] and [(OH)2-salophen)] on GQDs modified with SOCl2 was used from the comparison of the FT-IR spectra GQDs-Cl, GQDs-[(O)2-salen] GQDs-[(O)2-salophen] and GQDs-[Fe(O)2-salen] and GQDs-[Fe(O)2-salophen)] (Fig. 10 ). In the spectra of GQDs-[(O)2-salen] and GQDs-[(O)2-salophen] disappearance of the acyl chloride group signals (1711 cm−1 and 623 cm−1) and the appearance of the C=N peak at 1639 cm−1 and 1617 cm−1, represents the successful synthesis of Schiff base ligands GQDs-[(O)2-salen] and GQDs-[(O)2-salophen] (Fig. 10C and 10E). The ligands were attached to the substrate by the formation ester bond. Also, the shift of the azomethine band (C=NR) from 1639 cm−1 to 1591 cm−1 for the salen (and from 1617 cm−1 to 1601 cm−1 for the salophen) is due to the coordination of azomethine nitrogen atom to the metal in the complexes (Fig. 10D and F).
The EDX diagrams obtained (Fig. 11 ) show the presence of Fe (III) and cl ions in the structure of GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe -Salophen].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of cell apoptosis in vitro

ce:section.ce:para: The amount of cell apoptosis in MCF7 cancer cells treated with different combinations of GQDs-[Fe-Salen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] was measured by flow cytometry analysis of annexin V/PI labeled with FITC. As shown in Fig. 13 , the percentage of live MCF7 cells (annexin−/PI−) ranges from 39/12% for GQDs-[Fe-Salen] to 54.49% for GQDs-[Fe-Salophen], respectively. Also, GQDs-[Fe-Salen] can induce 35.77% early apoptosis (annexin+/PI−) and 17.49% late apoptosis (annexin+/PI+) in MCF7 cells, respectively. Meanwhile, the rate of apoptosis (both early and late) in cells treated with GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] is 19.41% (early apoptosis) and 16.36% (late apoptosis), respectively (Marino and Kroemer, 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell apoptosis assay by DAPI staining
ce:section.ce:para: Evaluating of nuclear morphology using DAPI staining is a valuable method to detect cell death by apoptosis or necrosis. The ability to induce apoptosis by the samples after MCF7 cells were incubated with them was studied using a DAPI analysis microscope. Microscopic images obtained for negative control (untreated MCF7 cells), MCF7 cells treated with GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] and GQDs-[Fe-Salen] compounds after 48 h of incubation are shown in Fig. 14 . Examining the microscopic images shows that under the same conditions, GQDs-[Fe-Salen] leads to more fragmentation of cells than GQDs-[Fe-Salophen]. Control cells have normal nuclei and do not show significant chromatin degradation. So, in general, from the results of DAPI staining, it can be concluded that GQDs-[Fe-Salen] compared to GQDs-[Fe-Salophen] can prevent the growth of MCF7 cells more effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of cell uptake in vitro

ce:section.ce:para: Here we proposed two methods for synthesizing and stabilizing [Fe-salen] and [Fe -salophen] complexes on the GQDs. Then, antitumor activity, cell death mechanism, cell uptake GQDs-[Fe-salen] and GQDs-[Fe-salophen] upon MCF7 cells were evaluated. This study shows that treatment of MCF7 cells with different concentrations of GQDs-[Fe-salen] and GQDs-[Fe-salophen] cause inhibits cell growth in one pattern dependent on time and dose. In the present study, flow cytometry analysis by annexin V-FITC/PI on MCF7 cancer cells indicates cell death of kind apoptosis. For evaluation and confirm the results of MTT- assay and annexin V-FITC/PI was used the DAPI staining. Results in vitro show the GQDs-[Fe-salen] in comparison with GQDs-[Fe-salophen] cause nuclear aggregate, cell morphological changes and ultimately more cell death of MCF7 cancer cells. According to the cell uptake results, GQDs-[Fe-salen] is significantly absorbed by MCF7 cells and these confirm the results obtained from MTT assay. In general can be said parameters such as cellular permeability and solubility, may play a role in the amount of cytotoxicity of GQDs-[Fe-salen] and GQDs-[Fe-salophen].

Zahra Kiani Nejad: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ali Akbar Khandar: Project administration, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Massoumeh Khatamian: Project administration, Investigation. Marjan Ghorbani: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
